coredata.dc:title: Thin carbon films: Correlation between morphology and field-emission capability
coredata.dc:creator: Gabdullin, Pavel
Zhurkin, Alexey
Osipov, Vasiliy
Besedina, Nadezhda
Kvashenkina, Olga
Arkhipov, Alexander
coredata.dc:description: The paper presents a new study of correlation between morphology of thin carbon films deposited on Si substrates, their electronic properties and capability of cold electron emission in electric field with macroscopic magnitude of the order of 1 V/μm. Comparative experiments have demonstrated that substrate parameters have little, if any, direct effect on emission properties of the films: the observed variations of the emission threshold field value in the range from several percent up to two times depending on the substrate species was attributed to substrate-orientation-sensitive mechanism of the film growth leading to notable difference in topography of the compared films (indirect effect). Tunneling spectroscopy study showed that the films with the best emission properties comprise sp 2 carbon islands (or carbon dots, CDs) having electron systems separated from each other and from the substrate. This conclusion has been supported by the presence of a photoluminescence component in Raman spectra of the emissive films, which is often associated with electron confinement effects in CDs. In combination, the new experimental data witness in favor of hot-electron mechanism of low-threshold electron emission from the studied films.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thin carbon films
Island films
Substrate effect
Carbon dots
Field electron emission
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of substrate on emission properties

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors express their gratitude to S.K. Gordeev (Central Institute for Materials, Petersburg, Russia) for the provided film samples and useful discussions. N.A. Besedina thanks for financial support the SPbAU RAS state order, project 16.9790.2017/BP .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Emission current characteristics of carbon films deposited on Si substrates of different types:
b) samples on KDB-10 substrates (doped with B, 10 Ω·cm) with surface orientations: (111) and (100).
The insets show the same data plotted in Fowler-Nordheim coordinates.
To the assumed structure of emitting (left) and non-emitting (right) thin carbon films on silicon.
AFM images of carbon films on different Si substrates (the same as in Fig. 1a):
a) n + (KEF-4.5); b) n-type (KEF-7.5); c) p-type (KDB-12); d) p + (KDB-1).
STM image of a film sample on p +-Si substrate (KDB-1, see Fig. 3 d).
Tunneling I-V curves (integral tunneling spectra) measured at three typical spots of a carbon film sample on p +-Si substrate KDB-1 (see topography in Figs. 4 and 3 d).
a) Spot 1 – in a topography recession between carbon islands, in comparison with a typical tunneling I-V curve for a clean KDB-1 wafer etched to remove oxide layer;
b) Spot 2 – at one of the islands; the inset shows and smoothed characteristics near zero point.
c) Spot 3 – at another carbon island; four I-V curves measured in the dark conditions.
d) An I-V curve measured at the same spot 3 when the sample was exposed to ambient light.
Averaged Raman spectra for three locations on the carbon film sample deposited on (100)-4° Si substrate. Inset: G and D carbon peaks enlarged.

A fragment of Raman spectrum for a non-emitting sample and its three-peak Voigt (Gaussian-Lorentzian) fitting. The dotted red line represents the fitting result, the solid black line is the experimental curve. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Averaged Raman spectra for different locations on carbon film samples on Si substrates with (100) and (111) orientation. The dotted lines indicate the expected positions of sp 2 carbon peaks G and D.
Parameters of emission sites estimated from emission current characteristics.

head.ce:title: Thin carbon films: Correlation between morphology and field-emission capability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Peter the Great St. Petersburg Polytechnic University, Physical Electronics Dept., 29 Politekhnicheskaya, St. Petersburg 195251, Russia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Peter the Great St. Petersburg Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Peter the Great St. Petersburg Polytechnic University, Physical Electronics dept., 29 Politekhnicheskaya, St. Petersburg, 195251, Russia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: St Petersburg Academic University — Nanotechnology Research and Education Centre RAS, 8/3A Khlopin St., St. Petersburg 194021, Russia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: St Petersburg Academic University — Nanotechnology Research and Education Centre RAS

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: St Petersburg Academic University — Nanotechnology Research and Education Centre RAS, 8/3A Khlopin st. , St. Petersburg, 194021, Russia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The paper presents a new study of correlation between morphology of thin carbon films deposited on Si substrates, their electronic properties and capability of cold electron emission in electric field with macroscopic magnitude of the order of 1 V/μm. Comparative experiments have demonstrated that substrate parameters have little, if any, direct effect on emission properties of the films: the observed variations of the emission threshold field value in the range from several percent up to two times depending on the substrate species was attributed to substrate-orientation-sensitive mechanism of the film growth leading to notable difference in topography of the compared films (indirect effect). Tunneling spectroscopy study showed that the films with the best emission properties comprise sp 2 carbon islands (or carbon dots, CDs) having electron systems separated from each other and from the substrate. This conclusion has been supported by the presence of a photoluminescence component in Raman spectra of the emissive films, which is often associated with electron confinement effects in CDs. In combination, the new experimental data witness in favor of hot-electron mechanism of low-threshold electron emission from the studied films.

ce:section.ce:para: Many species of carbon films and nanostructured materials with relatively smooth vacuum boundaries were reported to emit electrons at room temperature in electric field with low macroscopic magnitude (of the order of 1 V/μm), and many different hypotheses were suggested to explain this phenomenon [1–6]. One of such theories describes electron transfer into vacuum by tunneling through multiple (two or more) potential barriers – at the surface and internal boundaries. This mechanism was proposed initially for diamond or DLC films [7–9] with conductance-band states of sp 3-hybridized carbon serving as intermediate electron states. Emission facilitation in this model is achieved due to the action of electric fields (intrinsic and/or external) penetrating into non-conductive components of the cathode structure and transferring energy to electrons, thus producing a hot-electron population that easily permeates the surface barrier. Relevance of this model for various smooth-surface carbon emitters has been debated for decades [10,11]. Recent experimental results gave additional arguments in its favor – and not only with respect to sp 3-C structures. For instance, the paper [12] reports on observation of an additional broad peak in the spectra of electrons emitted from nanographitic films, shifted by a few eV to low energies, which “evidences electron emission through a dual potential barrier” [12]. Earlier, such films were comprehensively studied by A.N. Obraztsov and co-authors [13,14] and were found to consist of carbon in the conductive sp 2 state. This is especially remarkable because high conductivity was usually considered as an obstacle for field penetration into a material and thus for generation of hot carriers. Another evidence of the relevance of the hot-electron model for nanographitic films with relatively smooth outer boundary can be found in the article [15] describing experiments on laser-assisted field electron emission. According to the mechanism suggested in [15], the energy of incident laser pulses was converted into the thermal energy of semi-isolated electronic subsystem which resulted in generation of long-living hot electrons eventually emitted from the material.

On the other hand, coexistence of sp 2 and sp 3 nanodomains separated by sharp boundaries is known to enhance low-field electron emission capability of carbon materials [1,2,4,6]. A recently discovered new form of nanocarbon – so-called quenched carbon (or Q-carbon) produced by the pulsed laser annealing (PLA) method – comprises small sp 2-clusters embedded in sp 3-matrix and is reported to possess outstanding mechanical and electric properties [16,17]. Tetrahedra of the sp 3-hybritized carbon presumably form emission centers (ECs) in this material while the presence of sp2-bonded of carbon clusters facilitates emission by inducing localized electron states near the ECs' Fermi level. Electrons occupying these states face a reduced potential barrier for tunneling into vacuum. Additional local field enhancement is achieved due to electrical heterogeneity of the material [2,16].

Field-emission properties of graphitic carbon films were studied in previous experiments of our laboratory [18–20]. Films were deposited onto crystalline Si wafers and had smooth outer boundary and effective (averaged) thickness of the order of 1 nm or less. The films consisting of separate islands with nanoscale lateral dimensions (which can be classified as carbon dots, CDs) – unlike continuous coatings – were found to emit electrons at room temperature in electric field with macroscopic magnitude as low as ~1 V/μm. Investigation of surface topography and electrostatic simulations has showed that the local field enhancement at either outer or internal boundaries cannot be sufficient for one-barrier Fowler-Nordheim electron emission from the studied structure. At the same time, the localization (confinement) effects obstructing electron cooling in nanosized crystallites [21–23] could have created beneficial conditions for the hot-electron (dual-barrier) mechanism of electron emission. In recent papers [20,24], we have suggested that the intrinsic electric field providing energy to hot-electron population may be of thermoelectric nature and originate from the heat flow propagating from emission centers into the substrate. The experiments reported in this paper were aimed at verification and refinement of the proposed model. We investigated correlation between emission capability of thin carbon film samples and details of their morphology and electronic structure.

As in the previous works [18–20], we used samples of thin carbon films on naturally oxidized crystalline Si wafers provided by S.K. Gordeev (Central Institute for Materials, St. Petersburg, Russia) whose group fabricated them by chemical decomposition of acetylene. To investigate influence of the substrate on emission properties, we chose substrates with different conductance and surface orientations. Emission characteristics of the samples were measured in vacuum with residual gas pressure of the order of 10−7 Torr. Emission current was extracted onto a flat-top 6 mm-diameter cylindrical Mo anode separated from the sample by a 0.5–0.6 mm-wide vacuum gap.

Atomic force microscope (AFM) Pacific Nanotechnology NanoDST was used to assess the surface topography. Its finer details were studied by means of vacuum scanning tunneling microscope (STM) Proton-MIET SM-2000-Vac operated in I = const mode. This instrument was also used in the spectroscopic (STS) mode to investigate local surface conductivity and local density of electron states (DOS) near the Fermi level at selected locations. For this purpose, dependencies of tunneling current vs probe-sample bias I(U) were recorded for a series of gradually reduced probe-to-surface distances h. At some point, the effect of h on I(U) plots saturated, hence, the current was not limited by the tunneling gap. According to STS theory [25], the derivative dI/dU calculated from a saturated I(U) characteristics represents the local DOS distribution. Since the characterized films showed certain photosensitivity, the STM surface topography assessment was performed in the dark conditions – to exclude the photocurrent contribution weakly depending probe-to-surface distance. During STS measurements, the effect of optical illumination on I(U) plots was specially investigated.

Raman studies were made with Horiba Jobin-Yvon LabRam HR800 instrument with 1800 g/mm grating in the backscattering geometry. The incident beam of a 532 nm laser with maximum output 50 mW was focused onto a 2 μm target spot. The data were recorded in Raman shift range (100–7000) cm−1. To improve signal-to-noise ratio, the data were averaged over 10 or 20 measurements, thus full data acquisition process for each location took 10 through 20 s. As recommended by the instrument manufacturer, the recorded spectra were calibrated using a standard Si sample. To eliminate the dark current contribution to the data, special ‘dark measurements’ were performed for each sample and the obtained curves were subtracted from ‘raw’ spectra recorded with the laser beam on.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of substrate on emission properties
ce:section.ce:para: Electron emission from a thin film can be strongly affected by the substrate and by the film-substrate interface. Therefore, we investigated the correlation between emission properties of the film samples and the Si substrate species.

Four carbon film samples were fabricated simultaneously in one CVD deposition process on Si wafers doped with different elements (boron or phosphorus) in different concentrations. The macroscopic field F was defined by dividing the applied voltage by the field gap width. It should be mentioned that emission was normally unstable immediately after the initial emission turn-on and noticeable perturbations in current-voltage characteristics near the threshold were very usual. At this stage, only a few most efficient local emission centers were active. When a higher voltage was applied to extract higher current, these “early” centers usually extinguished and emission redistributed more uniformly, current characteristics stabilized and became smoother. We have not yet made any comprehensive study of the emission stability of the investigated films, however, other carbon-based coatings, for example, the abovementioned Q-carbon, are known to be capable of producing stable (within ~2%) emission current over at least several hundred minutes in wide temperature and current density ranges [26].

Characteristics of emission current measured after their stabilization (“weathering”) for film samples fabricated on Si wafers of different species are presented in Fig. 1 a . All these samples showed high emission capability – the field with macroscopic magnitude of F = 3.2 V/μm was sufficient to extract a measurable current in all cases. The turn-on field for the film on heavily doped n-type wafer was ~2 times lower than for the one on heavily doped p-Si, while the emission properties of the samples deposited on lightly doped substrates were intermediate.

The current dependencies in Fowler-Nordheim (FN) coordinates “ln(j/F 2) vs 1/F” (see inset in Fig. 1 a) were approximately linear, which is generally typical for field-emission current characteristics of nanocarbon materials [2,4,6]. Within the frames of the classic FN field emission theory, the slopes of such characteristics give us a combination of workfunction φ field enhancement factor β for the emitting site, namely, (φ3/2/β). Taking plausible workfunction for carbon φ = 3.5–4.5 eV [19,20], we can evaluate the field enhancement factor required to reconcile the measured emission characteristics with the FN theory. Such estimates for the studied samples are listed in the column “β eff ” of Table 1 , together with the corresponding data from literary sources. In all cases, these β eff values have the order of ~100 or greater. However, according to the results of numeric simulations [19], such values cannot be justified for the thin carbon films on silicon composed by the islands having the observed sizes and spacing – unlike, for instance, the case of Q-carbon [16] where high local field enhancement can, in principle, originate from volumetric electric heterogeneity. This discrepancy is very common for nanocarbons and suggests that the FN emission model where electrons are transferred into vacuum by tunneling through a single potential barrier is not fully relevant for this class of materials [2,4,6].

The current characteristics in Fig. 1 b represent emission properties of two film samples fabricated on p-type wafers KDB-10 with identical volumetric properties but different surface orientations: parallel to dense crystallographic planes (111) and (100). These two samples were fabricated in one CVD process (the same reactor load), together with a sample on n-type wafer KEF-4.5 cut at 4° with respect to (100) plane (denoted as (100)-4°). As it was established in [20], the utilized CVD process produces discontinuous carbon films (disordered CD arrays) on oxidized (111) and (100) Si surfaces – in accordance with the Volmer-Weber film growth mode [32]. In the case of (100)-4° wafers with high concentration of structural surface defects (atomic steps, etc.), Stranski-Krastanov growth mechanism [32] leads to formation of carbon islands on top of a continuous graphene-like (one-monolayer thick [20]) carbon coating. Such films were incapable of low-field electron emission. Results of emission testing of the fabricated samples fully met these expectations. The films deposited on densely packed (111) and (100) Si emitted electrons in macroscopic fields starting from ca. 1.5 and 1.1 V/μm, respectively (Fig. 1 b). This minor difference could be attributed to different morphology of the deposited films (see in [18]). The (100)-4° sample produced no measurable emission current in fields up to 5 V/μm, in agreement with the previous experimental results reported in [20]. Fig. 2 illustrates the assumed difference in the structure of the emitting and non-emitting carbon coatings.

The noted discrepancy in emission thresholds between film samples fabricated on dissimilar wafers in the same CVD process could have been attributed to the expected substrate effect. However, we consider this effect as indirect, i.e. caused by a difference in the deposited films' morphology witnessed by the AFM images in Fig. 3 . The sample having the lowest emission threshold (Fig. 3 a) consists of the largest and distinctly separated carbon islands. The worst emitter (Fig. 3 d) comprises smaller and practically merging CDs. According to our previous experimental results [18–20] and to the model proposed in [20,24], only discontinuous thin carbon films are capable of low-field emission.

This assumption was verified by emission testing of carbon films with island morphology (similar to the one shown in Fig. 3) deposited on conductive surfaces. For this purpose, thin carbon films were grown by the standard CVD technique on wafers with removed (etched out) oxide layer. As in the previously described case, the CVD film growth process followed Stranski-Krastanov mode and the substrate between the islands was covered with a continuous conductive sp 2-C layer. Consequently, no low-field emission was observed. The same was correct for thin carbon films on metallic (tantalum) foils. On the other hand, the films deposited on undoped Si wafers also showed poor emission parameters. Thus, the presently available experimental data agree with the hot-electron emission mechanism demanding both: 1) penetration of electric field into the emitter structure, and 2) sufficiently easy electron transport to the surface-located emission centers.

A typical STM image of the sample on p +-Si wafer is shown in Fig. 4 ; its comparison with the AFM image in Fig. 3 d confirms their general compliance. It should be noted, though, that the STM studies of thin discontinuous films on weakly conducting substrates were seriously hindered by charging effects. For this reason, among the samples characterized in Figs. 1, 3 the STM images were successfully acquired only for a sample on the substrate having the highest conductivity, which corresponded to the worst emission capability.

For this sample, tunneling (STS) spectra were measured at lateral positions related to different topographic features seen in STM images. These spectra divided into three main groups (Fig. 5 ). Spectra of the first group were measured at topographic recesses and reveal the band structure of the substrate (Fig. 5 a). The “shelf” in the tunneling I-V curves corresponds to ~1 eV-wide bandgap with the Fermi level situated near its low-energy edge (for p-type substrate). Valence and conduction bands are represented by the inclined plot sections at higher negative and positive biases, respectively. Effect of optical illumination on the tunneling spectra of this group (see in Fig. 5 a) was insubstantial.

Approximately symmetrical tunneling spectra attributed to the second group (Fig. 5 b) were recorded at elevated parts of STM images, i.e. at carbon islands' tops. The spectra show practically continuous DOS with zero-width or narrow bandgap – in any case, not exceeding ~300–400 meV. Consequently, the carbon islands can be classified as somewhat “oversized” graphene quantum dots (GQD) [33–35], in good agreement with the microscopic images and with the available data on their phase (sp 2/sp 3) composition [20].

Exposure of the samples to optical radiation (for instance, ambient light of the laboratory) resulted in notably increased slope near the zero point in the tunneling I-V curves (see in Fig. 5 b). This effect can be explained by generation of photoexcited charge carriers which can also be responsible for the previously observed photoconductivity of such films [19].

Though, the tunneling I-V curves recorded at certain island-top locations were characterized by distinctly different shapes and behavior which allowed their attribution to a separate group. In the dark conditions, they carried high-magnitude noise component and included large intervals where the signs of current I and probe bias U were opposite (Fig. 5 c). These features were reproducible over many successive bias sweeps. Exposure to light had the strongest effect onto such I-V characteristics – their shapes became smoother and much more similar to those for other film islands (Fig. 5 d). However, the curves remained notably displaced with respect to the zero point.

We believe that the tunneling I-V curves of the third group (Fig. 5 c,d) relate to “electrically insulated” CDs, having their electron system separated from the substrate and from other carbon islands. Their irregular dark-current characteristics result from electrical charging during probe bias sweeps. Exposure to light breaks their separation and simultaneously induces photovoltaic and/or thermoelectric effects enhanced by weak electron-phonon coupling and low electronic heat capacity inherent in GQDs [36]. Remarkably, the I-V plot showed in Fig. 5 d crosses horizontal axis at |U| ≈ 0.3 V, which coincides with the photovoltage value determined in [19] by means of the conventional contact technique.
For comparative Raman studies, we chose carbon film samples on Si substrates with different surface orientations: (100), (111) and (100)-4°, described in Section 3.1.

Figs. 6, 7 and 8
represent averaged Raman spectra for a few different locations on each of the studied samples. The spectra for the non-emitting sample on (100)-4°-oriented substrate (Fig. 6) were highly reproducible and informative. The peaks near 520 cm−1 and 950 cm−1 are characteristic for silicon. Low width of the 520 cm−1 peak indicates perfect crystallinity of Si wafer; its dominating magnitude implies high transparency (i.e. low effective thickness) of the carbon coating. Other features of the spectra (D, G, D', 2D, D + D' [34,37,38]) are directly related to sp 2 carbon. The central frequency of G peak is close to 1580 cm−1: for example, fitting of the spectrum represented by black line in Fig. 6 with Voigt profile using Origin 8.5 gives G peak centrum position 1583 cm−1. The following features of the acquired spectra should also be noted:
−
D peak is present at 1340÷1345 cm−1 and has high relative magnitude: I
D /I
G = 1.6÷1.75;
−
G and D peaks are broadened (65÷75 cm−1);
−
the “secondary” peaks D' (at 1611 cm−1) and D + D' are present;
−
however, the peaks G and D' do not completely merge and can be distinguished separately: see, for instance, a spectrum fitting result in Fig. 7 where a small peak appears at the D' position 1610 cm−1.

The acknowledged scheme for classification of carbon compounds by Raman data has been suggested in [37,39] and describes three basic stages of spectrum transformation between fully crystalline sp 2 and fully amorphous carbon. By the features listed above, the spectra in Figs. 6, 7 should be attributed to the early second stage, during which the G peak shifts from ~1600 cm−1 to ~1520 cm−1 and the I
D /I
G ratio drops from approximately 2 to about 0.25 as the sp 3 content rises from zero to ~20% [39]. Hence, in the assessed carbon coating the sp 3-phase is present only in a minor amount, most probably not exceeding 10%.

The spectra may be also compared, for instance, with those obtained in [40,41] for thin films of reduced graphene oxide (RGO) deposited by PLD technique on oxidized n-type silicon. The latter also have pronounced D, G and 2D peaks at approximately the same positions. Though, the ratio I
D /I
G is lower and is does not exceed a unit for majority of the studied samples, which can evidence of lower defect concentration in the PLD-deposited RGO films in comparison with the CVD carbon films studied in this work. In [40], the seed graphene layer produces a sharp and intense 2D peak at or below 2700 cm−1, known to be characteristic for single-layer graphene [34]. The 2D peaks in the spectra in Fig. 6 are broader and have lower relative magnitudes. Consequently, these spectra are most probably related to the CDs [34] and not to the graphene monolayer which (as it was established in [20]) fully covers (100)-4° Si substrates at early stages of the CVD process.

The determined value of I
D /I
G can be used to estimate the characteristic size of sp 2 domains of the CDs. For this purpose a few alternative formulas were suggested [37,41,42], basing on the approach originally developed by Tuinstra and Koenig [43]. For the spectra in Fig. 6 all these formulas give close values ~10 nm, in good agreement with the microscopic data on typical CD sizes (Figs. 3, 4 and [18–20]). For example, the expression from [37] gives the following estimate:
L D
2
=
4.3 ·
10 3
/
E L
4
·
I D
/
I G
− 1
≈
4300 /
2.33
4
·
1.7
− 1
≈ 85.8
nm 2
,
where L
D is average interdefect distance which in the considered case can be associated with characteristic lateral dimension of sp 2 domains; E
L is excitation laser photon energy expressed in eV. This yields L
D ≈ 9.3 nm, which is close to the value (9.81 nm) determined in [42] for a PLA-grown RGO thin film on WC-Co substrate.

Concluding, the spectra presented in Figs. 6, 7 identify the carbon component of poorly emitting samples as nanocrystalline graphite (nanographite) – as opposed to graphene, graphite or amorphous carbon.

In contrast, Raman spectra of well-emitting samples on Si wafers with (100) or (111) surface orientation showed essentially different shapes (Fig. 8). Magnitudes of the characteristic carbon peaks were close to or below the noise level, i.e. were substantially reduced in comparison with the previously considered case, even though the presence of sp 2 carbon on the surface was reliably confirmed by alternative methods (STM, STS and others [20]). At the same time, many of the Raman spectra for these samples possessing the low-macroscopic-field emission capability included wide-band photoluminescence (PL) peaks with magnitudes, shapes and extrema positions varying from spot to spot and from sample to sample (see in Fig. 8). Suppression of the characteristic carbon peaks and appearance of the PL component may have common origin in obstruction of electron-phonon interaction in the CDs by confinement effects which could also be responsible for the enhanced low-field emission ability of the studied films [24].

Carbon dots of different types are known to show high PL capability, even when their core DOS apparently does not have a bandgap of sufficient width [33–35,44,45]. The concrete mechanism of this phenomenon remains unclear, though it is often associated with slowed relaxation of exited electrons [33,34]. On the other hand, slowed relaxation of excited electrons represents one of necessary conditions for efficient hot-electron (or dual-barrier) emission [6]. Numerous papers (for instance, [46–48]) reported that presence of a graphene or graphene oxide layers in contact with photoluminescent nanoparticles leads to PL quenching. This phenomenon was explained by the transfer of photoexcited carriers or excitation energy to the carbon structure. Assuming the hot-electron mechanism of low-macroscopic-field electron emission for the studied CD films, one could expect that the presence of graphene or its derivative on the (100)-4°-oriented substrates would efficiently suppress emission from such film samples.

The reported experiments yielded new experimental facts on the morphology of thin carbon films on oxidized silicon substrates possessing the property of low-field electron emission. The performed comparative emission tests have shown that the degree and the type of substrate doping have little or no effect on the emission capability of deposited films – provided that substrate conductivity was sufficient for charge transport to the emitting regions. In contrast, substrate surface orientation can have substantial (even though indirect) influence on emission capability of the deposited films affecting their morphology.

The performed AFM, STM/STS and Raman spectroscopy studies showed that the emissive films consist of nanosized (≥10 nm in lateral dimensions, 1–5 nm in height) islands of sp 2 carbon which have electronic properties similar to those of GQDs. In the dark, electron systems of these islands (at least, some of them) are separated from their environment. Optical illumination induces electric connections and (relatively weak) photoluminescence in the films having enhanced field-emission capability. These observations imply increased relaxation times of excited electrons in the carbon islands comprising such films, thus witnessing in favor of the hot-electron (or dual-barrier) mechanism of low-field electron emission from such films [6,20,24].

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Pavel Gabdullin:Writing - review & editing.Alexey Zhurkin:Writing - review & editing.Vasiliy Osipov:Writing - review & editing.Olga Kvashenkina:Writing - review & editing.Alexander Arkhipov:Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest regarding the publication of this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors express their gratitude to S.K. Gordeev (Central Institute for Materials, Petersburg, Russia) for the provided film samples and useful discussions. N.A. Besedina thanks for financial support the SPbAU RAS state order, project 16.9790.2017/BP.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanisms of electron field emission from diamond, diamond-like carbon, and nanostructured carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Robertson, Mechanisms of electron field emission from diamond, diamond-like carbon, and nanostructured carbon, J. Vac. Sci. Technol. B 17 (1999) P. 659–665, doi:10.1116/1.590613.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Low-macroscopic-field electron emission from carbon films and other electrically nanostructured heterogeneous materials: hypotheses about emission mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.G. Forbes, Low-macroscopic-field electron emission from carbon films and other electrically nanostructured heterogeneous materials: hypotheses about emission mechanism, Sol. St. Electron. 45 (2001) 779–808, doi:10.1016/S0038-1101(00)00208-2.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Field emission from products of nanodiamond annealing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.V. Okotrub, L.G. Bulusheva, A.V. Gusel'nikov, V.L. Kuznetsov, Yu.V. Butenko, Field emission from products of nanodiamond annealing, Carbon 42 (2004) 1099–1102, doi:10.1016/j.carbon.2003.12.007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel cold cathode materials and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.S. Xu, S. Ejaz Huq, Novel cold cathode materials and applications, Mater. Sci. Eng. R-Rep. 48 (2005) 47–189, doi:10.1016/j.mser.2004.12.001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electron field emission from undoped polycrystalline diamond particles synthesized by microwave-plasma chemical vapor deposition

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Nose, R. Fujita, M. Kamiko, Y. Mitsuda, Electron field emission from undoped polycrystalline diamond particles synthesized by microwave-plasma chemical vapor deposition, J. Vac. Sci. Technol. B 30 (2012) 011204, doi:10.1116/1.3670988

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Field emission from carbon nanostructures: models and experiment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.D. Eidelman, A.V. Arkhipov, Field emission from carbon nanostructures: models and experiment, Phys. Usp. (2019) accepted paper, doi:10.3367/UFNe.2019.06.038576.
M.W. Geis, J.C. Twichell, T.M. Lyszczarz, Diamond emitters fabrication and theory, J. Vac. Sci. Technol. B 14 (1996) 2060–2067, doi:10.1116/1.588986.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen containing hydrogenated amorphous carbon for thinfilm field emission cathodes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The use of internal field emission to inject electronic charge carriers into the conduction band of diamond films: a review
